title: Chapter 17: At the Gates of Fading Eternity
paragraphs: Agdash loomed out of the ashen fog like the picked-over skeleton of a gargantuan beast that refused to die. Its colossal walls, erected upon the foundations of three long-vanished cultures, were overwhelming. The rough, heavy masonry of the lower tiers, preserving the traces of the elder forging, transitioned into elegant but cracked marble spires, thickly inscribed with ancient rune script. These runes, which once pulsed with the gold of mana, now barely smoldered with a sickly yellow light, desperately trying to hold back the breath of the Void.
The wind carried with it the scent of rotting ozone and cold stone dust. The sky above the city resembled an old, leaden bruise.
Four travelers slowly approached the Great Gates. Their footsteps muffled as they sank into the gray ash of the wastes.

Yashil walked ahead. The small green-skinned figure against the backdrop of the monumental architecture of the end of the world seemed insignificant, but the goblin's step was steady. In his wide, dark eyes, there was no fear. There, contrary to all logic of the rotting reality, lived a quiet, stubborn hope. He believed that behind these impregnable walls, where the remnants of civilization took shelter, a place for dialogue would be found. That the weariness of death would force the nations to forget their old hatreds.

Behind him, treading heavily and leaving deep furrows in the ash, walked Kirah. The massive demon of the Legion, whose body of black pitch was crossed by crimson, pulsing veins, was trembling. This trembling was not caused by the cold of the wastes. Kirah shrank, slouching his vast, spine-covered shoulders, instinctively trying to appear smaller. He was panically afraid of this city. He feared the crowd, he feared the judging elven eyes.

"They will kill us," the demon whispered hoarsely, a vibrating thread of sound, without lifting his head. "I can smell their fear. And where there is fear, steel always sings. I... I don't want to lose control, Yashil. If they strike, my darkness may answer on its own."

"A wolf doesn't think about what the sheep will say when he comes out of the forest," Janavar philosophized, toying with a heavy, jagged axe. The werewolf stepped springily, lazily rolling from heel to toe. His wolfish muzzle was twisted into a familiar, slightly contemptuous smirk. "Let them just try to bare their iron. I'll show them who's the quote and who's the paragraph here. Remember, brothers: if the wolf is silent, it's better not to interrupt him. Auf."
Despite the bravado, Yashil noticed how the werewolf's amber eyes tensely scanned the battlements on the walls, and the fur on his scruff stood on end.

Renée walked last, elegantly stepping over clumps of ash in her torn stockings. She carelessly held a long mouthpiece, leaving behind a trail of greenish, tart smoke that mingled strangely with the smell of Armageddon.

"You three are charming idiots," the fox drawled melancholically, releasing a perfect smoke ring. "We look like the hallucination of a dying drunkard. A pacifist demon, a werewolf with a superiority complex, a prodigal fox, and a goblin who fancies himself a messiah. My dear green friend, the world doesn't work by the laws of children's fairy tales. These walls were built to keep out those like us. They will die of hunger inside, but they will not open the gates for us. That is the nature of things."
"The nature of things changes when there is nowhere left to go," Yashil replied quietly but firmly.
They stopped fifty paces from the gates.

The guard of Agdash noticed them from afar. The air above the wall filled with sharp, guttural commands. In the battlements, the heads of heavy crossbow bolts instantly flashed, and the runes on the gate leaves flared with an aggressive, warning crimson light. High elves in soot-stained but once magnificent gold armor lined up in a monolithic formation before the entrance. Their faces were haggard, their eyes sunken, but their hands gripped their halberds with the fanatical strength of the doomed.

The captain of the guard stepped forward. A long crimson scar crossed his pale face, disappearing under his collar. He scanned the procession with a look full of such crystal-clear, undiluted disgust that it felt almost tangible.
"Stand, filth!" his voice, amplified by an ancient spell, struck their ears like a whip. "One more step toward Great Agdash, and you'll be turned into pincushions for arrows."

Kirah whimpered softly, backed away, and instinctively raised his massive paws, covering his horned head. This gesture of submission from a four-meter monster looked so unnatural that several elves exchanged confused glances.
"Take it easy, long-ears," Janavar snarled, baring his fangs and gripping his axe with both hands. "Don't wake a sleeping beast. We came in peace, but my axe is always ready for a dialogue."

"Shut your maw, mutt," the captain spat, gripping the hilt of his sword. His gaze flicked from one outcast to another. "I see before me the spawn of Zakhan, a filthy half-wolf, a harbor tart of the beastfolk, and..." the captain faltered, staring at Yashil. His face contorted in a wicked grimace. "And a goblin. Even the Void did not spawn such a wretched joke. Get back to the wastes before we burn you with sacred fire."
Renée elegantly flicked the ash from her cigarette, casting a long, significant look at Yashil that read a cold: "I told you so."

The atmosphere tightened to a ringing limit. One wrong move, one sharp breath—and dozens of crossbow bolts would have pierced their bodies. Kirah squeezed his eyes shut, preparing to receive the pain. The werewolf tensed his leg muscles for a deadly lunge.
But Yashil did neither.

The small goblin calmly, without haste, lowered himself to his knees directly into the gray, radioactive ash before the elven spears. He did not reach for a weapon he did not have. He did not start shouting curses or begging for mercy.

He looked the captain straight in the eye. In the goblin's gaze, there was neither the rage of the rejected nor the humiliation of a slave. There swirled a bottomless, quiet sadness of a being who sees another's pain more clearly than his own.

"I see your scars, captain," Yashil said loudly enough for every elf in the formation to hear. "You are tired of burying your brothers. You look at the heavens and wait for the end. We are not enemies. We are the same shards of this world with nowhere left to go. We do not ask for your bread, and we do not ask for your bed. We ask only for the right to stand with you on the same earth when the darkness comes."

The captain was taken aback. In his logic of the universe, a goblin should have either fled in cowardice or pounced like a rat driven into a corner. Such humility, bordering on absolute, indestructible dignity, shattered all stereotypes.
"You... you don't understand who you're talking to, green-skinned scum," the elf's voice wavered, losing part of its iron confidence. "You will not enter. Never."
"Then we will wait," Yashil answered simply. And he remained sitting on his knees, looking at the barred Great Gates.
The wind of the wastes howled with new force, raising clouds of ash.

Renée sighed, adjusted the elven cloak slipping from her shoulder, and, gracefully lowering herself to the ground beside the goblin, crossed her legs. "A monstrous waste of time," the fox whispered, taking a drag. "But the view from here is quite dramatic."

Janavar swore loudly and with feeling in the language of the forest tribes, drove his huge axe into the ground with a swing, and sat down with his legs crossed, leaning his back against the shaft of the weapon. "A wolf doesn't wait for handouts," he grumbled. "A wolf just... conserves energy. Yes. Auf."

Kirah opened his eyes. Seeing his friends sitting calmly before an army of armed elves, the demon stopped trembling for the first time in weeks. He lowered himself heavily to his knees behind Yashil, turning into a huge, living, impenetrable black shield, covering the small goblin from the cruel wind of fading eternity.

They did not break the doors, and they did not ask for mercy. Four outcasts simply remained sitting at the gates of the end of the world, and the silence that hung over Agdash became louder than any battle horns.
